coredata.dc:description: Lignin is the most abundant, but underutilization polyphenolic material on the earth. In this paper, an integrated platform for pH sensing and ions separation was prepared from lignin to make use of this abundant bioresource. Demethylation of enzymatic hydrolysis lignin was first achieved to mimic the catechol structure of dopamine. The demethylated lignin was then converted into carbon dots by a facile hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared carbon dots emit green fluorescence with enhanced quantum yield, and exhibited pH-responsive color changes from yellow-green to blue. When incorporated into a chitosan aerogel, the pH-responsive property of the carbon dots was intact, whereas the adsorption capacity of the aerogel for Cu2+ ions was noticeably improved, which was owed to the abundant oxygen containing functional groups and unique optical properties of the as-prepared carbon dots. This study provides a feasible approach to convert lignin into fluorescent nanomaterials for pH sensing and metal ions recovery from wastewater.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical modification of lignin by demethylation, and characterization of the demethylated lignin. Notes: Lignin means the raw lignin applied; de-Lignin means the demethylated lignin.

(A) Preparation of CDs from demethylated lignin; (B) XPS survey, (C) C1s, (D) O1s, and (E) S2p high-resolution spectra of CDs; (F) FT-IR spectra of CDs and demethylated lignin; (G) The size distributions histogram of CDs; and (H) TEM image of CDs.

(A) Absorption, exaction and emission spectra of C-CDs; (B) 2D fluorescence topographical map of C-CDs; (C) Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of GL-CDs; (D) 2D fluorescence topographical map of GL-CDs.

(A) Photographs of GL-CDs solutions with different pH values under the UV-light; (B) Emission spectra of GL-CDs solutions at different pH values under the exaction wavelength of 365 nm; (C) Photographs of dropping simulated wastewater of different pH values onto GL-CD/aerogels under UV light. Notes: raw means the aerogel without addition of any droplets.

(A) Effect of initial pH value on the adsorption capacity of GL-CDs/chitosan to Cu2+ ions, adsorption kinetics; (B) High-resolution O1s XPS spectra of GL-CDs/ chitosan before and after the adsorption of Cu2+ ions and adsorption isotherms; (E) Fitting results of the pseudo-first-order model; (F) Pseudo-second-order model; (G) Langmuir model; (H) Freundlich model; (I) Reusability of GL-CDs/chitosan for Cu2+ ions removal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignin is the most abundant, but underutilization polyphenolic material on the earth. In this paper, an integrated platform for pH sensing and ions separation was prepared from lignin to make use of this abundant bioresource. Demethylation of enzymatic hydrolysis lignin was first achieved to mimic the catechol structure of dopamine. The demethylated lignin was then converted into carbon dots by a facile hydrothermal treatment. The as-prepared carbon dots emit green fluorescence with enhanced quantum yield, and exhibited pH-responsive color changes from yellow-green to blue. When incorporated into a chitosan aerogel, the pH-responsive property of the carbon dots was intact, whereas the adsorption capacity of the aerogel for Cu2+ ions was noticeably improved, which was owed to the abundant oxygen containing functional groups and unique optical properties of the as-prepared carbon dots. This study provides a feasible approach to convert lignin into fluorescent nanomaterials for pH sensing and metal ions recovery from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The extensive utilization of copper (Cu) in various industries, such as electrical, lightening, machinery manufacturing, construction, and preservation, along with inadequate management of Cu-containing wastewater and solid waste, has resulted in severe environmental pollution (Chen et al., 2021; Mi et al., 2012; Pan et al., 2021). Consequently, it is crucial to develop efficient strategies for mitigating the contamination of water ecosystems by copper ions in order to ensure their sustainability. Among various techniques, adsorption is a promising method for copper ion removal from water due to its simple operation, excellent removal efficiency, and economic affordable (Awad et al., 2019; Chai et al., 2021; Mozaffari Majd et al., 2022). Nevertheless, the adsorption behavior of copper ions is greatly influenced by the acidic or alkali environment of the aqueous solution, which is attributed to the pH-dependent changes in adsorbent surface charge and the speciation of copper ions in solution (Huang et al., 2019; Safavi-Mirmahalleh et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2021). Therefore, accurate measurement of pH value is vita in determining the effectiveness of the adsorption process.

Carbon dots (CDs) are quasi zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with particle size below 20 nm (Liu et al., 2020a). They have found tremendous applications in a wide range of fields because of their superior properties, such as optical properties, high biocompatibility, high stability, cell penetrability, and electron mobility (Lamba et al., 2024; Zhang et al., 2022). Because of the presence of alterable functional groups, such as amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl groups, CDs is believed to be an advanced materials for hazardous ions removal from waste water (Wang et al., 2022). In the study by Perumal et al. (2022), CDs were synthesized from Red Malus floribunda fruits by hydrothermal carbonization method, and be further embedded into spherical hydrogel particles by an inverse emulsion process. The CDs-doped hydrogel particles can remove heavy metal ions, including Hg(II), Cd(II), Pb(II), and Cr(III) from multiple systems efficiently with 99 % removal efficiency of Hg(II) ion (Perumal et al., 2022). In another study by Xie et al. (2021), a unique fluorescent CD-magnetic nanocomposite is synthesized by hydrothermal method, which recovers >96.5 % of Hg(II) from Chagan Lake water, tap water and drinks (Xie et al., 2021). The mechanism is attributed to the interaction oxygen and nitrogen containing reactive hydrophilic groups of CDs and heavy metal ions (Li et al., 2023a), which results in formation of a non-fluorescent complex as is convinced by fluorescence quenching of CDs after metal ions chelating (Xie et al., 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs) prepared from lignin is a potential low-cost and environmentally friendly material for visual detection of pH due to the different protonation degrees of their surface functional groups at different pH values (Liu et al., 2020b; Yang et al., 2021a; Zhao et al., 2020). However, the reported lignin-based CDs usually emit blue light within the wavelength range of 380–460 nm, with low quantum yield (QY) around 3 %, which reduces its application value as pH sensing (Kang et al., 2020). Extensive research has been conducted to enhance the optical properties of lignin-based CDs (Li et al., 2023b; Wan et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2023). The QY plays essential role in confirming the brightness of emitted light. Lignin-based CDs of high QY is desirable to enhance their potential applications in pH sensing and other fields, including biological imaging, energy harvesting and display technologies (Shi et al., 2019; Wei et al., 2022). The introduction of electron-rich functional groups has been extensively employed to improve the QY value of lignin-based CDs, which facilitates electron delocalization and suppresses non-radiative decay (Liu et al., 2020a; Xu et al., 2020). Zhao et al. (2021) have successfully improved the QY of lignin-based CDs from 1.65 % to 5.9 % by incorporating hydroxyl and amino groups.

On the other hand, the emission wavelength directly influences the fluorescence color and functionality of lignin-based CDs. For example, the fabrication of multicolor lignin-derived carbon dots is achieved by a stepwise solvent-fractionation engineering with dynamic ethanol sieving and dichloromethane/water extraction (Hou et al., 2023). Multicolor light-emitting CDs can selectively identify metal ions in intricate systems composed of Fe3+, Ag+, Cu2+, and Co2+ (Zhou et al., 2024). Lignin-based CDs with long emission wavelengths possess more pronounced color change, which display excellent application value in visual pH sensing (Zhu et al., 2019). Passivation of heteroatoms (Niu et al., 2017), pretreatment of lignin (Li et al., 2019) and adjustment of sp2 domains (Yu et al., 2021) have been developed to promote the emission wavelength of lignin-based CDs. Yang et al. (2021a) have tuned the emissive wavelength of lignin-based CDs to 510 nm by introducing Mg and N elements. The resulting lignin-based CDs with long emission wavelength displayed noticeable color change, imparting them with superior visual sensing capabilities. Despite considerable efforts have been achieved, lignin-based CDs with both high QY exceeding 10 % and long emission wavelength surpassing 500 nm remain relatively scarce.

Dopamine is an interesting molecule that can be used to develop advanced materials with desirable luminescence properties (Yang et al., 2020a). Zhang et al. (2012) was the first reporting polydopamine fluorescent organic nanoparticles that had tunable photoluminescence spectra, and showed potential applications in cell imaging. Because of the redox property of dopamine, it is an effective electron acceptor, and be transformed into o-dopaquinone derivative under oxidative conditions (Chai et al., 2015). Accordingly, dopamine was used to construct surface functionalized CDs with strong bluish green fluorescence that showed great potential in monitoring and medical diagnostics of tyrosinase/pH-associated disease (Chai et al., 2015). In another study, dopamine was found to quench the fluorescence at 610 nm, but recover the fluorescence at 420 nm of carbon dots-gold nanoclusters hybrid fluorescent probe (He et al., 2018). The successful application of dopamine in fluorescent material is due to catechol chemistry of the molecule that contributes to the redox properties and π-π stacking interaction of the resultant materials (Lin et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2012).

Lignin is a natural phenolic polymer primary comprising of 3-methoxyl-4-hydroxyphenylpropane derivatives (Zhang et al., 2023). Once demethylation, there will be pendent catechol groups in the lignin polymer, which may be used to simulate dopamine derived materials (Yang et al., 2021b). Herein, to tune the photoluminescence of lignin derived CDs for advanced applications, lignin was demethylated to afford polydopamine-like polyphenols with pending catechol groups. Then, the demethylated lignin was subjected to hydrothermal treatment to synthesize green emissive CDs with high QY. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs displayed fluorescence response to pH values that could be integrated into chitosan aerogel for metal ions recovery from wastewater.

The enzymatic hydrolysis lignin was provided by Longli Co., Ltd (Shandong, China). 1-Dodecanethiol (DT, AR), sodium methylate, phloroglucinol, chitosan (CS, AR) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR) were provided by Macklin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Glutaraldehyde (AR) and ethanol (95 % purity) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China).

Approximately, 20 mL 1-dodecanethiol, 80 mL DMF, and 13 g sodium methylate were mixed in a three-necked flask (250 mL) and stirred in an ice-water bath. Subsequently, 20 g lignin was added into the above solution, which was heated at 130°C by stirring for 60 min under a nitrogen atmosphere. The reaction was then terminated by adding 25 mL pure water into the mixture and cooling in an ice-water bath. The pH value of above mixtures was acidified to 2 with 10 wt% hydrochloric acid to precipitate lignin from mixtures. The precipitated lignin was obtained after centrifuging at 6000 rpm for 10 min and washing with distilled water for three times. The purified demethylated lignin powder was finally obtained after vacuum-drying at 50°C for 48 h. The content of phenolic hydroxy groups was determined by potentiometric titration according to previous reports (Qiu et al., 2009; Zhou et al., 2012).

Approximately, 0.1 g (demethylated) lignin and 0.02 g phloroglucinol were dispersed into 5 mL pure water and then 3 g H2SO4 was dropwise added into the above solution, which was performed in an ice bath. Subsequently, the mixtures were transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated up to 200°C for 10 h. After finishing the reaction, the resulting mixtures were filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane, followed by dialyzing against distilled water in a dialysis bag (100–500 Da) for 48 h. After freeze-drying the dialyzed solution for 72 h, the pure solid CDs powder was collected.

The 9,10-bis(phenylethynyl)anthracene with 87 % QY in ethanol solution was used as the standard to calculate the QY of CDs. The UV absorbance values of all the CDs samples were adjusted to 0.05–0.1 to minimize the inner filter effect. The fluorescence QY of all samples was determined at the optimal excitation wavelength. The QY of fluorescent CDs was calculated using the following Eq.
(1) QYX=QYR×(IX/IR)×(AR/AX)×(nX 2/nR 2)

where the subscripts R and X refer to the standard reference and sample solutions, respectively, QY represents the fluorescent quantum yield, I is the measured integrated fluorescent intensity, A represents the absorbance value, and n is the refractive index of the solvent.

The CDs/chitosan composite aerogel was obtained through a typical freeze casting method (Fan et al., 2021). Approximately, chitosan (1 g) was dissolved into 50 mL of acetic acid (1 wt%) and stirred at 500 rpm until homogeneous solution was formed. Subsequently, 20 mL glutaraldehyde solution (5 wt%) and 5 mL CDs aqueous solution (5 mg·mL−1) were added into the above solution, which was then vigorously stirred at 1000 rpm for 10 min by an OS40-S agitator (Dalong, Beijing). The mixture was then transferred into a mold and gelled at room temperature for 24 h to form the hydrogels. Finally, the resulting hydrogels were frozen-dried for 72 h to obtain the aerogels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption capacity of Cu2+ by CDs/chitosan aerogels
ce:section.ce:para: In the adsorption equilibrium experiments, 40 mL Cu2+ ion solution (25–250 mg/L) and 0.5 g CDs/chitosan aerogel were mixed in a 100 mL conical flask, which was then shaking at 25°C with a shaking rate of 150 rpm for 24 h. In the kinetic adsorption experiments, 40 mL of Cu2+ ion solution (200 mg/L) and 0.25 g CDs/chitosan aerogel were added into a 100 mL conical flask and shaking at 25°C under a shaking rate of 150 rpm. The suspension of the above mixture was collected at regular intervals for further analysis.

The concentration of Cu2+ in aqueous solution was analyzed according to the acetaldehyde-bis(cyclohexanone) oxaldihydrazone (BCO) method (Yu et al., 2013), which was performed by UV-Vis spectrophotometer (UV1006M031, Agilent, Japan) at 545 nm. All of the adsorption experiments were repeated for three times. The equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) was calculated using the following equation:
(2) qe=(C0-Ce)V/m
Where, C0, and Ce are the initial and final concentrations of Cu2+ (mg/L); V and m are the volume of Cu2+ solution (L) and weight of the CDs/chitosan aerogel (g), respectively.

The adsorption CDs/CS were shaken into 50 mL of 0.1 M HCl solution at 150 rpm for 3 h. Afterwards, the CDs/CS were removed and washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol to remove excess acid. After washing, the CDs/CS was dried under vacuum at 45 °C for 48 h and used in the next adsorption process.

The 2D-HSQC NMR spectra of lignin and demethylated lignin were collected on a Bruker AVIII 400 MHz NMR spectrometer (Bruker, USA). The elements states on the surface of CDs were analyzed on a Thermo Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron (XPS) spectroscopy (Thermo, USA). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of lignin and CDs were collected on a PerkinElmer Frontier Ⅱ spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The UV-Vis absorption spectra and the absorbance of CDs solutions were carried out on a UV1006M031 spectrophotometer (Agilent, Japan). Photoluminescence spectra of CDs were recorded on a fluorescent spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of CDs were observed with a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan).

The optical performance of CDs is significantly affected by the precursor structure (Cheng et al., 2021). Herein, polydopamine-like demethylated lignin was employed to prepare CDs with enhanced photoluminescence ( Fig. 1A). The demethylated lignin was synthesized by 1-dodecanethiol method according to previous report with minor modification (Sawamura et al., 2017), and be confirmed by potentiometric titration, XPS, and NMR studies. The results are displayed in Fig. 1. The phenolic -OH content of lignin increased noticeably from 3.21±0.14 mmol/g to 4.57±0.19 mmol/g after the demethylation modification (Fig. 1C), whereas the carboxylate content of the lignin remain intact (Fig. 1C), which indicated the success in preparation of demethylated lignin. To further confirm the structure of demethylated lignin, XPS studies were performed (Fig. 1D). The content of C element of lignin decreased to 31.08 % from 36.57 % after the demethylation, whereas the content of O element increased to 68.92 % from 63.43 %. There were 49.83 % of C element of the raw lignin linked by C-O bonds, whereas only 36.07 % of the C element of demethylated lignin were linked by C-O bonds (Fig. 1E). Similarly, the O1s high-resolution XPS spectra displayed a decrease in the C-O content from 73.63 % to 61.74 % after the demethylation of lignin (Fig. 1F). This phenomenon further convinced the removal of methyl groups from methoxy groups of lignin by the modification. In addition, 2D-HSQC NMR studies were also conducted to confirm the demethylation of lignin. The applied lignin displayed typical signals of guaiacyl, syringyl, p-coumrate, and ferulate units of grass lignin (Fig. 1G). The structure change of lignin upon demethylation could be assessed based on the integral ratios of the methoxy groups (OMe) and the γ-CH2OH contour signals (Sawamura et al., 2017). It was clear that the signal ratio of OMe/γ-CH2OH decreased by the treatment of lignin with 1-dodecanethiol (Fig. 1H). On the other hand, the aromatic methoxy groups were clearly visible after the demethylation. Similar results are also reported by previous study (Sawamura et al., 2017). These results suggested the partial demethylation of lignin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CDs from demethylated lignin
ce:section.ce:para: A facile hydrothermal method was employed to prepare CDs from demethylated lignin with the addition of sulfuric acid and phloroglucinol ( Fig. 2 A). Hereinafter, the CDs from demethylated lignin is denoted as GL-CDs, whereas the control CDs is denoted as C-CDs that is prepared from the raw lignin. XPS and FT-IR techniques were adopted to analyze functional groups of the as-prepared GL-CDs. The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 2B) displayed three obvious peaks at 531.3, 284.8, and 169.8 eV, corresponding to the O, C, and S elements, respectively (Yang et al., 2020b).The atomic percentage of O, C, and S elements were 47.65 %, 43.93 %, and 8.43 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2 C) displayed three peaks at 284.3, 286.7, and 288.9 eV, attributed to C-C/CC, C-S/C-O, and CO bonds, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D) displayed three peaks at 531.1, 532.3, and 533.4 eV, which are attributed to OC, O-S, and O-C bonds, respectively. The presence of C-S-O2, C-S-O3 and C-S-O4 in the as-prepared GL-CDs were confirmed by the peaks at 169.9, 168.4 and 167.6 eV displayed in the high-resolution S2p spectrum (Fig. 2E) (Wang et al., 2020). The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 F) of the as-prepared GL-CDs at 3430, 1627, 1129, and 623 cm−1 are distributed to the stretching vibrations of O-H, aromatic CC, C-S and C-CS, respectively (Wei et al., 2020), which certified the existence anionic sulfur-based functional groups in the GL-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2H, the GL-CDs particles were of quasi-spherical shape with size distribution in the range of 1–6 nm (Fig. 2 G). The average particle size of GL-CDs was determined to be 2.87 ± 0.026 nm by the statistical analysis. The GL-CDs displayed no obvious crystal lattices structure, which would be classified into polymeric carbonized dots (Xia et al., 2019).

The UV-Vis, excitation and emission spectra of the as-prepared CDs from demethylated lignin were shown in Fig. 3. The absorption band at the range of 260–280 nm is corresponded to the π-π* transition of aromatic CC, while the broad adsorption band at the range of 350–450 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition of CO and CS (Akash and Rehman, 2020; Hu et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2022). The 2D fluorescence topographical map of C-CDs and GL-CDs are shown in Figs. 3B and 3D, respectively. Both C-CDs and GL-CDs displayed an excitation-independent feature, since their emission peaks were located at 520 nm despite of the altering excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 420 nm. This excitation-independent feature was caused by the surface state after passivation of sulfuric acid (Ai et al., 2021). Both C-CDs and GL-CDs exhibited optimal excitation is 365 nm. However, the emission curve of C-CDs and GL-CDs were significantly different. The C-CDs showed two obvious emission peaks at 420 and 520 nm (Fig. 3 A), respectively, whereas the GL-CDs exhibited an emission peak at 520 nm and a weak shoulder at 420 nm (Fig. 3C). In a previous report, polydopamine derived fluorescent nanoparticles also show emission spectra with emission peak at 520 nm (Zhang et al., 2012). In addition, dopamine is also reported to quench fluorescence emission of CDs because of the redox properties and π-π stack of the molecules (He et al., 2018). Therefore, the quenching of fluorescence emission at 420 nm of GL-CDs is attributed to the introduction of catechol group onto the demethylated lignins.

The QY of C-CDs and GL-CDs were 4.27% and 12.61%, respectively. The enhanced QY of GL-CDs could be attributed to the existence of additional electron-donating hydroxyl groups on the surface of GL-CDs, which promoted the radiative recombination of electrons and holes (Yan et al., 2019). In addition, the increased hydroxyl groups also facilitated the crosslink enhanced emission effect of GL-CDs, a carbonized polymer dots, further resulting in an increased QY value (Xia et al., 2019). The above results revealed that introduction of catechol functional groups to the demethylation of lignin was critical to improve the optical properties of resultant CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH sensing behavior of CDs derived aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: The specific fluorescence response of the as-prepared GL-CDs to pH values in range of 2–9 is shown in Fig. 4. The fluorescence color of GL-CDs solutions remained yellow with no substantial variation within pH range of 2–3. Further elevation of pH value from 3 to 5 resulted in the attenuation of yellow fluorescence. Interestingly, the fluorescence color changes to blue while increasing pH value from 5 to 7, whereas the fluorescence of GL-CDs solutions diminished gradually and eventually disappeared with pH value increasing from 7 to 9. The emission spectra of GL-CDs solutions at different pH values are shown in Fig. 4B. The GL-CDs exhibited dual-emissive peaks respective located at about 420 nm and 520 nm. The intensity of peak located at 520 nm decreased obviously when the pH value of solution increased from 2 to 6. Notably, the emission peak located at 520 nm was totally quenched at pH ≥ 5, which led to color changes of GL-CDs from yellow-green to blue. In addition, the fluorescent of GL-CDs solution was totally quenched when pH value was higher than 7. The intensity changes of the fluorescence peak of GL-CDs under different pH were attributed to the protonation/deprotonation of the oxygen containing functional groups, such as hydroxyl, sulfate groups. The above phenomena indicated the potential application of GL-CDs in visual detection of pH value of wastewater. To this end, GL-CDs were incorporated into a chitosan aerogel to prepare paper-like wastewater pH detector (Fig. S1). The pH sensing behavior of GL-CDs aerogel is shown in Fig. 4C. Color change of the aerogel was observed under UV light after addition of droplets with different pH values. The aerogel exhibited fluorescence properties akin to those of GL-CDs solution. When excited by UV-light, the color changes of aerogel under different pH were similar to that of GL-CDs. This experiment convinced the utilization of GL-CDs in pH sensing of acidic wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption behavior of Cu2+ by GL-CDs derived aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: The pH value of solutions could have a great effect on the adsorption of Cu2+ by aerogels. Therefore, the adsorption capacity of aerogels to Cu2+ in solutions with various pH values was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the results indicated that the addition of GL-CDs to chitosan aerogel could noticeably increase the absorption of Cu2+ ions by the aerogel. This was due to the high specific surface area of the GL-CDs and the abundance of the oxygen element containing functional groups of the GL-CDs. High-resolution O1s XPS spectra of GL-CDs/chitosan before and after the adsorption of Cu2+ ions were applied to illustrate the importance of oxygen containing functional groups (Fig. 5B). The intensity of the O-C peak (531.2 eV) significantly decreased after the adsorption of Cu2+ ions, indicating complexation between Cu2+ ions and oxygen-containing groups, such as -OH, and anionic sulfur-based functional groups (Fan et al., 2021). Increasing pH value of the solution, the equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) of both GL-CDs/chitosan and chitosan aerogel increased, which was attributed to the deprotonation of NH3 +, and the electrostatic interactions between -NH2 groups and Cu2+ ions. Interestingly, the adsorption capacity of GL-CDs/chitosan aerogel was as high as twice of that of chitosan aerogel, because of the presence of oxygen containing groups in GL-CDs, which did not undergo protonation under acidic conditions and facilitated the chelation of Cu2+ ions.

The adsorption kinetics of GL-CDs aerogel to Cu2+ was explored, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 C. The aerogels exhibited rapid adsorption of Cu2+. Both the GL-CDs/chitosan and chitosan aerogels reached maximum absorptions within 120 min. This is different from that of previously reported graphene oxide materials (Yang et al., 2010). Both pseudo-first-order (Fig. 5E) and pseudo-second-order (Fig. 5 F) kinetic models were adopted to further investigate the adsorption behavior of GL-CDs derived aerogel to Cu2+. The pseudo-second-order model was found better describing the adsorption of Cu2+ to the GL-CDs/chitosan aerogel with a correlation coefficient of 0.9972 than the pseudo-first-order model of 0.9892. This result indicated that chemical adsorption through strong electrostatic interactions was the primary driving forces of Cu2+ absorptions onto the aerogels. Accordingly, Langmuir and Freundlich models were further adopted to explore thermodynamics behavior of Cu2+ ions adsorption. The values of R2 from the fitting results of Langmuir model (Fig. 5 G) was higher than these of Freundlich model (Fig. 5H), suggesting that the adsorption behavior of Cu2+ was more aptly described by the Langmuir model. According to the analysis results of Langmuir model, adsorption capacities of Cu2+ by GL-CDs/chitosan aerogel were obviously higher than that of chitosan aerogel, which further convinced that the introduction of GL-CDs apparently enhanced the adsorption ability of Cu2+. Accordingly, the adsorption mechanism of Cu2+ ions by GL-CDs/chitosan was proposed. The presence of hydroxyl groups and NH2 in chitosan aerogel led to the absorption of Cu2+ ions by electrostatic attraction. The introduction of GL-CDs provided additional adsorption sites for Cu2+ ions because of the existence of oxygen containing functional groups and the high specific surface area of the particle that provides a mass transfer channel for the diffusion of Cu2+ ions (Shahnaz et al., 2020). Lastly, the reusability of the GL-CDs/chitosan aerogel for Cu2+ ions recovery was assayed (Fig. 5I). After 5 times of consecutive desorption-adsorption cycles, the removal efficiency of Cu2+ ions remained 91.7%, indicating excellent reusability of the GL-CDs derived aerogel.

In this paper, lignin was demethylated to provide catechol functional group that is analogous to dopamine molecule. The demethylated lignin can be used to enhance the quantum yield of carbon dots from lignin, because the introduced catechol groups contain additional oxygen than the original lignin. In addition, the demethylated lignin derived carbon dots exhibited intrinsic property of pH-response fluorescent behavior. Accordingly, an integrated sensing and separation platform was constructed via incorporating the carbon dots into a chitosan aerogel. The incorporation of carbon dots can significantly improve the absorption of Cu2+ by the aerogel, which can be described by Langmuir and pseudo-second-order models. The mechanism is ascribed to oxygen containing groups of the carbon dots that provide additional active sites. Overall, this manuscript provides a high value valorization of lignin as an integrated platform for pH sensing and metal recovery from wastewater.
